owasp_guidelines.notes: PASS: No passwords/tokens logged. Uses secrets.token_hex() for cryptographically random token generation. Session timeout implemented (1h JWT, 7d refresh). Account lockout not implemented but deactivation supported. Tokens properly hashed for storage (SHA256 in hash_refresh_token).

jwt_security.notes: PASS: Uses HS256 algorithm. JWT expiry set to 1 hour (appropriate). Required claims present (sub, email, role, exp, iat). Signature validated before payload access in verify_token(). No sensitive data beyond email/role in payload. Refresh tokens have separate type claim.

oauth_implementation.notes: FAIL: NO STATE PARAMETER for CSRF protection in OAuth flow. Token exchange is server-side (correct). Google token verified via googleapis endpoint. Domain restriction enforced server-side (@your-domain.com). Missing: state parameter generation/validation, PKCE support recommended.

refresh_token_security.notes: PARTIAL FAIL: Refresh tokens hashed using SHA256 (good). Token type validated ('refresh'). However: NO DATABASE STORAGE of refresh tokens for validation/revocation. Tokens only set in HTTP-only cookies but never checked against DB. No single-use or rotation implemented. Logout only clears cookie, doesn't invalidate token server-side. CRITICAL: No way to force logout or revoke sessions.

domain_restriction.notes: PASS: @your-domain.com enforced server-side in _is_allowed_domain() and verify_google_token(). Clear error message provided. Cannot be bypassed (checked after Google verification). Database constraint also enforces domain in User table.

rate_limiting.notes: FAIL: NO RATE LIMITING on auth endpoints. Auth routes have no throttling. Login attempts unlimited. No protection against brute force or credential stuffing. RECOMMENDATION: Add rate limiting middleware specifically for /api/auth/* endpoints.

error_handling.notes: PASS: Generic error messages used (TOKEN_EXPIRED, INVALID_TOKEN, USER_NOT_FOUND, ACCOUNT_INACTIVE, DOMAIN_NOT_ALLOWED). Consistent error format via HTTPException. Proper HTTP status codes (401, 403, 500). No stack traces sent to client (logger.exception used for server logs only).

audit_logging.notes: PASS: Login attempts logged (logger.info for successful login). Failed authentication logged via logger.warning for domain violations. User creation/login tracked in user_service. No secrets in logs (only email addresses logged). Uses structured logging. RECOMMENDATION: Add IP address logging for security events.

code_quality.notes: PASS: Type hints present throughout. Async/await used correctly with httpx.AsyncClient. No hardcoded secrets (loaded from settings). Clean separation: auth_service (business logic), user_service (data access), auth middleware (request handling), permissions (authorization), auth routes (API layer). Good docstrings.

xss_csrf_protection.notes: PASS: Tokens in Authorization header (not URL). Refresh tokens in HTTP-only cookies with secure flag in production. SameSite=lax cookie setting. CORS would be configured in main.py. No XSS vectors in auth flow. CSRF: OAuth lacks state parameter (see oauth_implementation).

runtime_validation.reserved_names: PASS - No SQLAlchemy reserved names used as fields
runtime_validation.async_patterns: PASS - No sync-blocking calls (time.sleep, requests) found
runtime_validation.type_hints: PASS - All functions have type hints

issues.description: Refresh tokens are not stored in database. No server-side session tracking or revocation capability. Logout only clears cookie but token remains valid until expiry.
issues.recommendation: Create refresh_tokens table to store hashed tokens with user_id, expiry, and revoked flag. Validate refresh tokens against DB on /refresh endpoint. Implement token rotation (invalidate old token when issuing new one).
issues.impact: Cannot force logout users. Compromised refresh tokens valid for 7 days with no revocation. Stolen refresh tokens can be used until expiry.

issues.description: OAuth flow missing state parameter for CSRF protection. Attacker could initiate OAuth flow and trick victim into completing it (OAuth login CSRF).
issues.recommendation: Generate random state parameter before redirecting to Google. Store in session/cookie. Validate state parameter matches on callback. Consider PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) for additional security.
issues.impact: Vulnerable to OAuth login CSRF attacks where attacker can link their account to victim's session.

issues.description: No rate limiting on authentication endpoints. Unlimited login attempts allowed.
issues.recommendation: Implement rate limiting (e.g., 5 attempts per 15 minutes per IP for /api/auth/google, 10 attempts per hour for /api/auth/refresh). Use Redis or in-memory store for tracking.
issues.impact: Vulnerable to brute force attacks, credential stuffing, and DoS on auth endpoints.

issues.description: hash_refresh_token() method exists but refresh tokens are never stored or validated in database. Method is unused.
issues.recommendation: Either implement full refresh token storage (preferred) or remove unused method to avoid confusion.
issues.impact: Code maintenance confusion. False sense of security.

issues.description: No IP address logging for authentication events. Failed login attempts not explicitly logged.
issues.recommendation: Add request.client.host to all auth logs. Log failed authentication attempts with IP. Consider structured logging with fields: {event: 'auth_failure', reason: 'invalid_token', user_id: null, ip: '1.2.3.4'}.
issues.impact: Difficult to detect brute force attacks or trace security incidents. No IP-based blocking possible.

issues.description: JWT contains user email and role in payload. While not highly sensitive, email is PII and role could leak privilege information.
issues.recommendation: Consider removing email from JWT payload (only store user_id). Frontend can fetch user details from /api/auth/me endpoint. Alternative: Use opaque tokens (random IDs) instead of JWTs for access tokens.
issues.impact: Minor privacy concern. JWTs can be decoded by anyone with token access.

issues.description: JWT_SECRET validation requires 32 characters but doesn't enforce complexity (entropy, character mix).
issues.recommendation: Add entropy check or require minimum hex/base64 length. Generate secrets using cryptographically secure methods: secrets.token_urlsafe(32).
issues.impact: Weak secrets could be brute-forced. Low risk if using proper secret generation.

issues.description: generate_token_jti() method exists but JTI (JWT ID) claim not added to tokens. Method appears unused.
issues.recommendation: Either implement JTI in tokens for revocation tracking or remove unused method. If implementing, add 'jti' to JWT payload and track in revocation table.
issues.impact: Code maintenance. JTI useful for token revocation if implemented.

security_findings.details: Refresh tokens stored only in HTTP-only cookies but never validated against database. No way to revoke tokens server-side.
security_findings.remediation: Implement refresh_tokens table with user_id, token_hash, issued_at, expires_at, revoked_at fields. Check DB on every token refresh.

security_findings.details: Missing state parameter in OAuth flow allows OAuth login CSRF attacks.
security_findings.remediation: Add state parameter: generate random value, store in session, validate on callback. Use constant-time comparison.

security_findings.details: Unlimited authentication attempts allow brute force and credential stuffing attacks.
security_findings.remediation: Add rate limiting middleware: 5 attempts/15min per IP for login, 10 attempts/hour for refresh. Return 429 with Retry-After header.

security_findings.details: Email address (PII) stored in JWT payload can be decoded by anyone with token.
security_findings.remediation: Consider removing email from JWT, only store user_id. Fetch user details via API when needed.

recommendations.recommendation: Implement server-side refresh token storage and validation
recommendations.rationale: Critical security gap. Cannot revoke sessions or track token usage. Stolen refresh tokens valid for 7 days.
recommendations.implementation: 1. Create refresh_tokens DB table. 2. Store hashed token on creation. 3. Validate against DB on /refresh. 4. Implement revocation endpoint. 5. Add token rotation (invalidate old on refresh).

recommendations.recommendation: Add OAuth state parameter for CSRF protection
recommendations.rationale: High-severity vulnerability per OWASP guidelines. OAuth login CSRF is well-documented attack.
recommendations.implementation: 1. Generate state = secrets.token_urlsafe(32) before redirect. 2. Store in secure cookie/session. 3. Validate state matches on callback. 4. Use constant-time comparison.

recommendations.recommendation: Implement rate limiting on auth endpoints
recommendations.rationale: Prevents brute force, credential stuffing, and DoS attacks. Standard security practice.
recommendations.implementation: 1. Add rate limiting middleware (Redis or memory-based). 2. Apply to /api/auth/* routes. 3. Return 429 with Retry-After header. 4. Different limits per endpoint.

recommendations.recommendation: Add IP address logging for all authentication events
recommendations.rationale: Essential for security monitoring, incident response, and detecting attack patterns.
recommendations.implementation: 1. Add request.client.host to all auth logs. 2. Log failed attempts. 3. Consider structured logging format. 4. Integrate with monitoring/alerting.

recommendations.recommendation: Implement account lockout after failed login attempts
recommendations.rationale: Defense-in-depth against brute force. Complements rate limiting.
recommendations.implementation: 1. Track failed attempts per user in DB or cache. 2. Lock account after 5 failures. 3. Require admin unlock or time-based unlock (30 min). 4. Send notification to user.

recommendations.recommendation: Consider implementing PKCE for OAuth flow
recommendations.rationale: Additional security layer for OAuth. Recommended for public clients. Protects against authorization code interception.
recommendations.implementation: 1. Generate code_verifier = secrets.token_urlsafe(32). 2. Calculate code_challenge = base64(sha256(code_verifier)). 3. Send code_challenge in auth request. 4. Send code_verifier in token exchange.

recommendations.recommendation: Remove email from JWT payload or use opaque tokens
recommendations.rationale: Reduce PII exposure in tokens. JWTs can be decoded by anyone.
recommendations.implementation: Option 1: Remove email from JWT, only include user_id. Option 2: Use opaque tokens (random IDs) with DB lookup. Store full session data server-side.
recommendations.effort: Medium (4-6 hours for opaque tokens, Low for removing email)

owasp_compliance.A01_broken_access_control: PARTIAL - Good RBAC but session revocation missing
owasp_compliance.A02_cryptographic_failures: PASS - TLS enforced, tokens hashed, proper key storage
owasp_compliance.A03_injection: PASS - No SQL injection (parameterized queries via SQLAlchemy)
owasp_compliance.A04_insecure_design: PARTIAL - OAuth CSRF vulnerability, no rate limiting
owasp_compliance.A05_security_misconfiguration: PASS - Secure defaults, HTTP-only cookies, domain restriction
owasp_compliance.A07_identification_authentication_failur: FAIL - No rate limiting, no account lockout, OAuth CSRF, no session revocation
owasp_compliance.A08_software_data_integrity_failures: PARTIAL - JWT signed but no JTI for revocation
owasp_compliance.A09_logging_failures: PARTIAL - Good logging but missing IP addresses
owasp_compliance.A10_ssrf: N/A - Not applicable to auth module

strengths: Strong JWT implementation with proper validation and expiry
Domain restriction enforced server-side with DB constraint
Good error handling with generic messages
Clean separation of concerns (service, middleware, routes)
weaknesses: No server-side session management or token revocation
No rate limiting on authentication endpoints
IP addresses not logged for security events
critical_gaps: Session revocation impossible - cannot force logout users
